coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/BiVO4 S-scheme piezo-photocatalysts improved carrier separation for efficient antibiotic removal
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Mingsong
Wang, Shihan
Shi, Haifeng
coredata.dc:description: Reasonable design of an efficient S-scheme photocatalyst remains an ongoing challenge due to the limitation of interfacial charge separation efficiency. Herein, CQDs/BiVO4 S-scheme heterojunction with Bi–O–C bond was synthesized by introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) growth on BiVO4 piezo-photocatalyst and absorbed visible light up to 750 nm. Under light + ultrasonic conditions, the reaction rate constant (k) of BVO/C-0.10 reached 0.0517 min−1 on tetracycline (TC) degradation, which was 2.24 and 4.04 times higher than those of BiVO4 and CQDs, respectively. The enhanced performance was attributed to the improved efficiency of the photogenerated carrier separation, originating from the combination of piezoelectric effect and S-scheme heterojunction with Bi–O–C bond. The Bi–O–C bond at the CQDs (3–8 nm) and BiVO4 interfaces connected the two semiconductor materials and provided an atomic-level interface channel for carrier migration. The piezoelectric properties of the composites were investigated by piezo−response force microscopy (PFM). Based on Mott−Schottky curves, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and scavenging experiments, the possible piezo-photocatalytic mechanism was proposed in combination with the band structures and characteristics of CQDs and BiVO4. This work furnishes unique insights into developing efficient S-scheme piezo-photocatalysts for purifying wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
BiVO4
Piezo-photocatalysis
S-scheme heterojunction
Tetracycline

Prof. Haifeng Shi was indebted to the financial support from the Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis of CQDs/BiVO4, (b) XRD patterns of BiVO4 and BVO/C samples, and (c) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, BiVO4, and BVO/C samples.
(a) SEM image of BiVO4, (b, c)TEM image of BVO/C-0.10, (d) HRTEM image of BVO/C-0.10, and (e) EDS elemental mapping of BVO/C-0.10 sample.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorbance spectra of the BiVO4, BVO/C samples, and CQDs and (b) tauc plots.
(a) Survey scan and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) V 2p, (c) Bi 4f, (d) C 1s, and (e) O 1s.
(a) PFM amplitude butterfly loops and (b) PFM phase hysteresis loops of BVO/C-0.10.

(a−c) The catalytic activity of BiVO4, CQDs, and BVO/C samples under ultrasonic vibration. (d−f) The catalytic activity of BiVO4, CQDs, and BVO/C samples under light irradiation. (g−i) The catalytic activity of BiVO4, CQDs, and BVO/C samples under light + ultrasonic.
Possible pathways of CQDs/BiVO4 in light degradation of TC are proposed.
Acute toxicity for (a) Fathead minnow and (b) Daphnia magna, (c) developmental toxicity, and (d) mutagenicity of TC and its degradation intermediates.

The Mott−Schottky plots of (a) BiVO4 and (b) CQDs. (c) EIS spectra of CQDs, BiVO4, and BVO/C-0.10 and (d) photocurrent response plots of CQDs, BiVO4, and BVO/C-0.10 under light/dark. (e) Piezo-photocatalysis degradation efficiencies of BVO/C-0.10 after adding radical quenchers and (f) reusability of BVO/C-0.10 for degradation of TC for four consecutive cycles.

Energy band structure changes (a) before contact, (b) after contact, and (c) contact under light irradiation between CQDs and BiVO4. (d) Piezo-photocatalytic degradation mechanism of TC using BVO/C-0.10.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/BiVO4 S-scheme piezo-photocatalysts improved carrier separation for efficient antibiotic removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNational Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reasonable design of an efficient S-scheme photocatalyst remains an ongoing challenge due to the limitation of interfacial charge separation efficiency. Herein, CQDs/BiVO4 S-scheme heterojunction with Bi–O–C bond was synthesized by introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) growth on BiVO4 piezo-photocatalyst and absorbed visible light up to 750 nm. Under light + ultrasonic conditions, the reaction rate constant (k) of BVO/C-0.10 reached 0.0517 min−1 on tetracycline (TC) degradation, which was 2.24 and 4.04 times higher than those of BiVO4 and CQDs, respectively. The enhanced performance was attributed to the improved efficiency of the photogenerated carrier separation, originating from the combination of piezoelectric effect and S-scheme heterojunction with Bi–O–C bond. The Bi–O–C bond at the CQDs (3–8 nm) and BiVO4 interfaces connected the two semiconductor materials and provided an atomic-level interface channel for carrier migration. The piezoelectric properties of the composites were investigated by piezo−response force microscopy (PFM). Based on Mott−Schottky curves, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and scavenging experiments, the possible piezo-photocatalytic mechanism was proposed in combination with the band structures and characteristics of CQDs and BiVO4. This work furnishes unique insights into developing efficient S-scheme piezo-photocatalysts for purifying wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: For the sustainable development of society, catalysts using solar energy for photocatalytic reactions are considered one of the most promising solutions to current environmental problems in the high ecological risks of antibiotic contamination [1–8]. Notably, introducing a piezoelectric potential generated in the photocatalysts can offer improved photocatalytic performance [9–13]. BiVO4 with piezoelectric activity is adapted to high-efficiency piezo-photocatalytic reactions, which proves that BiVO4 is an excellent piezo-photocatalytic material. Wang et al. [14] demonstrated that the high piezoelectric property of BiVO4 is better catalytic performance compared with the low piezoelectric property of BiVO4 under light + ultrasonic conditions, which was because the piezoelectric polarization can separate the carriers effectively. However, BiVO4 piezo-photocatalytic performance is limited owing to the high conduction band position and unsatisfactory carrier separation, which makes it challenging to improve the piezo-photocatalytic efficiency for the degradation of tetracycline (TC) [15]. Strategies to optimize piezo-photocatalytic performance were extensively studied, including constructing heterojunctions, loading precious metals, introducing defect levels, and controlling morphology [16–18]. In the above strategies, the construction of heterojunction can effectively overcome the limitations of BiVO4 piezo-photocatalysis. The construction of heterojunction can improve the charge separation efficiency and redox ability of photocatalysts, especially in the S-scheme heterojunction [19–21].

In the S-scheme heterojunction, electrons transfer when two semiconductor materials with different Fermi levels come into contact, and an internal electric field (IEF) and band bending are generated at their interface [22–29]. Conspicuously, the internal electric field formed at the interface not only separates the photogenerated carriers on the material surface but also recombines the photogenerated electrons and holes with the weak oxidation ability, leading to the carriers with the strong redox ability being retained. Recently, Hao's group [30] reported that constructing a CdS/BiOCl S-scheme heterojunction could improve the redox ability and charge separation efficiency of the catalyst. More recently, Yu's group [31] found that the S-scheme heterojunction can promote the photocatalytic reaction, and the Ni–O bond in the S-scheme heterojunction can achieve fast charge transfer. It is necessary to develop BiVO4-based S-scheme heterojunctions with excellent photocatalytic performance [32,33]. Nevertheless, most of the reported piezo-photocatalytic materials and S-scheme heterojunction catalysts still exhibit the following problems: lack of research on interfacial interaction at the chemical bond level and poor light response.

Currently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are extensively applied in catalysis as a promising material owing to their excellent light absorption, ease of preparation, non-toxicity, and favorable water solubility, which can generally promote the separation of charge carriers in the catalyst [34–45]. In recent studies, CQDs are applied as semiconductors to build heterojunctions and hence improve photocatalytic performance. For instance, Yang et al. [46] constructed CQDs/PpPD heterojunctions with efficient photocatalytic performance. Recently, Zang's group [47] reported that the Z-scheme heterojunction constructed by CQDs and Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets can effectively degrade BPA. More recently, CQDs/Bi2WO6 S-scheme heterojunctions were developed for boosting antibiotic degradation due to the interfacial interaction of the Bi–O–C bonds, which could provide an atomic-level interfacial channel for boosting charge separation [48]. Nevertheless, up to now, there are few reports concentrated on constructing S-scheme tailoring carrier transfer performances to boost piezo-photocatalysis.

Here, we construct a novel S-scheme CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction, which consists of growing CQDs on the surface of the BiVO4 piezoelectric material. Notably, C–O–Bi bonds were formed in the S-scheme heterojunction, which allowed CQDs and BiVO4 to structure a tighter connection, thus providing an atomic-scale interface channel for charge transfer. In addition, due to the piezoelectric field generated by polarization charges of BiVO4, the photogenerated electrons inside the BiVO4 were transferred to the surface of the material, which were more easily transferred to the CQDs through the C–O–Bi bond on the BiVO4 surface. Consequently, the degradation activity of CQDs/BiVO4 on TC was significantly enhanced under light + ultrasonic conditions. The results of CQDs/BiVO4 piezo-photocatalysis revealed that it was worth studying to improve the photocatalytic performance by combining the above strategies.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), carbamide (H2NCONH2), glucose anhydrous (C6H12O6), and nitric acid (HNO3) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd. (China).
Detailed information on synthesis methods is presented in Fig. 1 (a) and Text S1 in Supporting Information.
Detailed information on the material characterization was presented in Text S2.
Detailed information on the degradation experiment of tetracycline was provided in Text S3.

The crystal structures of BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 composites were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), as displayed in Fig. 1(b). The patterns of BiVO4 coincide well with the monoclinic BiVO4 standard card (PDF#97-010-0604). The growth of carbon dots did not affect the crystallinity of BiVO4, and the modification of CQDs resulted in no diffraction peaks other than BiVO4, which may be due to the amorphous nature of CQDs [49]. The molecular structure of the photocatalysts was identified by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1(c), absorption bands at 735 and 1380 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching mode of Bi–O, and absorption bands at 820 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching mode of V–O, appear in both BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 samples [50]. In particular, the peaks of CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs near 1600, 1356, and 1000 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, and C–O, respectively, compared with pure BiVO4, which indicated the interaction between CQDs and BiVO4 [51–53].

The morphological characteristics of the samples were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and High-Resolution (HR)TEM. In Fig. 2 (a), SEM could observe small pieces of BiVO4. The TEM image of BVO/C-0.10 was exhibited in Fig. 2(b, c), in which CQDs with diameters ranging from 3 to 8 nm were attached to small pieces of BiVO4 [54]. As shown in Fig. S1, the particle size of CQDs was 1 to 6 nm. The lattice fringe structure of BVO/C-0.10 was observed in the high-resolution TEM image. The lattice spacing was 0.221 nm in Fig. 2(d), corresponding to the plane (121) of BiVO4. EDS elemental mapping indicated the existence of C, Bi, V, and O elements in the BVO/C-0.10 sample (Fig. 2(e)). Fig. 3 (a) presents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs, BiVO4, and BVO/C samples. The absorption edge of pure BiVO4 was around 520 nm, and it could absorb part of the light. CQDs exhibited excellent light absorption between the 400 and 750 nm wavelength, which could utilize most visible light. The introduction of CQDs increased the light absorption of BiVO4 at the wavelength of the specific band edge length, and the absorbance in the visible region gradually enhanced with the increase of the content of CQDs, which indicated that the CQDs improved the response of BiVO4 to visible light [55,56]. As illustrated in Fig. 3(b), the band gap values of BiVO4 and CQDs were 2.30 and 1.63 eV, respectively, which were calculated by equation αhν = A(hν−E g) n
/2 [54,57].

The surface elements and their valence states of BVO/C-0.10 piezo-photocatalyst were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4(a), the XPS survey spectrum showed obvious signals of Bi, V, O, and C elements. Fig. 4(b) shows two peaks at 516.5 eV (516.7 eV) and 523.9 eV (524.4 eV) corresponding to the V5+ oxidation state of BVO/C-0.10 (BiVO4) [58]. Fig. 4(c) shows two peaks at 158.9 eV (159.1 eV) and 164.3 eV (164.4 eV) that were attributed to the BVO/C-0.10 (BiVO4) binding energy of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, which indicated the existence of Bi3+ [59]. The C 1s XPS spectrum of BVO/C-0.10 was displayed in Fig. 4(d). The result revealed the CC at 284.5 eV, the C–O at 285.3 eV, the C–O–Bi band at 286.5 eV, and the CO at 288.5 eV. The spectrum of O 1s in Fig. 4(e) shows fitted peaks of BiVO4 appearing at 529.8 and 530.9 eV, which was assigned to the lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen. In addition, BVO/C-0.10 exhibited fitted peaks at 529.7, 530.6, 531.8, and 532.8 eV, which were assigned to the lattice oxygen of the lattice oxygen, adsorbed oxygen, CO band, and C–O–Bi band, respectively [60]. The Bi 4f, V 2p, and O 1s peaks of BVO/C-0.10 were negatively shifted compared to BiVO4, revealing BiVO4 tendency to acquire electrons [61–63]. As shown in Fig. 5(a), a typical amplitude voltage butterfly curve from −13 to 13 V was obtained to illustrate the strain change arising from the applied electric field, demonstrating the piezoelectricity of the BVO/C-0.10. In Fig. 5 (b), after reversing the polarization direction, the hysteresis lines exhibited a switching of about 160°, which confirmed that BVO/C-0.10 was a ferroelectric material by piezo-response force microscopy (PFM) [64].

The performance of the prepared piezoelectric catalysts was evaluated by degradation experiments on TC solutions under ultrasound [65,66]. As showcased in Fig. 6 (a–c), the piezoelectric catalytic activity of the composites was lower than that of the BiVO4 monomer due to the growth of carbon quantum dots on the surface of BiVO4, which reduced the stress on BiVO4. The performance of the prepared piezo-photocatalysts was investigated by degradation experiments on TC solution under light. As shown in Fig. 6(d−f), the photocatalytic activity of the composites was better than that of CQDs and BiVO4. The performance of the prepared piezo-photocatalysts for the degradation of TC was investigated under light + ultrasonic conditions. As depicted in Fig 6(g−i), the BVO/C-0.10 material exhibited an excellent degradation rate of 0.0517 min−1, which was 2.24 times higher than BiVO4 under light and ultrasound, respectively. Notably, the BVO/C-0.10 composite piezo-photocatalytic degradation rate was 1.53 times higher than photocatalysis (0.0336 min−1) and 47 times higher than piezoelectric catalysis (0.0011 min−1), respectively, which demonstrated the synergistic effect between piezoelectric catalysis and photocatalysis. In addition, the degradation information of this work and similar studies was plotted in Table S1, and BVO/C-0.10 showed a high TC degradation efficiency.

LC−MS detection in Fig. S2 revealed the TC degradation pathway of BVO/C-0.10 to gain a deeper understanding of the piezo-photocatalytic degradation process of TC. Fig. 7 preliminarily proposed pathways for removing TC. In the pathway Ⅰ, TC was hydroxylated to produce P1 (m/z = 462) under the action of hydroxyl free radicals. The subsequent formation of P2 (m/z = 301) was attributed to the deamidation and cleavage of the carbon ring of P1, followed by the cleavage of the carbon ring of P2 into P3 (m/z = 400). The product P4 (m/z = 175) was derived from hydroxyl stripping and ring cleavage of P3. With the progress of degradation, P4 further cracked carbon rings to form P5 (m/z = 151) and P6 (m/z = 74). In pathway Ⅱ, TC was demethylated to form P7 (m/z = 413). After removing the N-methyl substituents of P7, the intermediate product P8 (m/z = 342) was formed, and then the ring-opening reaction was carried out to transform P9 (m/z = 222). In addition, considering the formation of P9 (m/z = 222), two carboxyl groups were removed based on TC9 to obtain P10 (m/z = 118) and P11 (m/z = 90). Pathway Ⅲ dehydroxylates TC to obtain P12 (m/z = 427) and then dehydrates to produce P13 (m/z = 411). P13 produced P14 (m/z = 279) through ring-opening reaction and addition reaction, then removed hydroxymethyl and diethylamino to obtain P15 (m/z = 166), and further ring-opening of P15 to obtain P16 (m/z = 131) and oxidation to produce P17 (m/z = 106).

The toxicity of TC products was evaluated during CQDs/BiVO4 piezo-photocatalytic degradation (Fig. 8 ), and the toxicity of degradation products to Daphnia magna and Fathead minnow was assessed by TEST to represent their impact on the environment. As could be seen from Fig. 8(a), the LC50-96 h of the original TC for Fathead minnow was 0.9 mg L–1, showing high toxicity. No product in TC degradation pathway Ⅰ was significantly more toxic than the original TC. In degradation pathway Ⅱ, the toxicity of the product P7 was higher than that of TC and then decreased. Only P13 was more toxic than TC in intermediates produced by Pathway Ⅲ. The product of all paths was harmless. As shown in Fig. 8(b), LC50-48 h of the original TC against Daphnia magna was 8.7 mg L–1. In the degradation pathway Ⅰ, the toxicity of all intermediates except P4 was reduced. For degradation pathway Ⅱ, P7 was more toxic than TC, indicating that they were more toxic to Daphnia magna, but the end product was significantly less toxic. In pathway Ⅲ, all intermediates except P12 and P13 were less toxic than TC, especially P16 and P17, reaching harmlessness. The above results showed that CQDs/BiVO4 not only exhibited a solid ability to degrade TC but also could significantly reduce the toxicity of TC products. The developmental toxicity and mutagenicity of TC and its degradation products to organisms were evaluated. It could be seen from Fig. 8(c, d) that the developmental toxicity and mutagenicity of the final products of each pathway were lower than that of TC.

Mott−Schottky plots were tested to clarify the flat band potential (E fb) as well as band structures for BiVO4 (Fig. 9 (a)) and CQDs (Fig. 9(b)) samples. The BiVO4 and CQDs photocatalysts were a kind of N-type semiconductor because of their positive slope of the linear region [67]. The Ag/AgCl electrode potential was transformed to normal hydrogen electrode potential (NHE) based on the equation E(NHE) = E(Ag/AgCl) − E θ + 0.059 pH (E θ (Ag/AgCl, at pH = 7) = 0.197 V) [68]. The E fb of n-type semiconductor material generally was 0.2 eV below the CB potential [69]. Therefore, the CB of BiVO4 was calculated to be 0.37 V, and the VB potential was determined to be 2.67 V. The CB of CQDs was calculated to be −0.89 V, and the VB potential was determined to be 0.74 V [70]. The migration and recombination properties of photogenerated charges were studied through photoelectrochemical measurements [71]. Fig. 9(c) shows that the semi-arc diameter of BVO/C-0.10 was smaller than that of CQDs and BiVO4, indicating that BVO/C-0.10 exhibited lower interfacial charge transfer resistance. As shown in Fig. 9(d), the BVO/C-0.10 material exhibited a stronger photocurrent signal than the CQDs and BiVO4 material, indicating that photogenerated carriers transferred and reacted faster [72–76].

The trapping experiment was one of the standard methods to explore the primary free radicals in the photocatalytic reaction process. In Fig. 9(e), the degradation results of TC on BVO/C-0.10 composite under light + ultrasound were exhibited. The BQ, t-BuOH, and EDTA were used as scavenging agents (0.1 mol L–1) for •O2 −, •OH, and h+, respectively, which were added to the system to remove the corresponding free radicals [77,78]. Compared with the experiment without the scavenger, the degradation efficiency of TC in the ·OH and h+ scavenging experiment was reduced, speculating that •OH and h+ played a significant role in the piezo-photocatalytic degradation process. In the •O2 − scavenging experiment, the degradation performance of BVO/C-0.10 on TC decreased slightly, indicating that •O2 − was also involved in the piezo-photocatalytic degradation. If the photogenerated electrons in the CQDs conduction band were transferred to the conduction band of BiVO4, the electrons in the conduction band of BiVO4 were challenging to produce •O2 −. As a result, electrons in the BiVO4 conduction band would tend to recombine with h+ produced by light in the valence band of CQDs, which was in line with the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism instead of type II. Recycling experiments were performed on the recovered BVO/C-0.10 sample to evaluate the reusability of the BVO/C-0.10 sample [79–81]. As illustrated in Fig. 9(f), after four degradation experiments, a slight decrease in the decomposition efficiency of TC was found, which confirmed the reusability of the piezoelectric catalyst. Relevant literature revealed that the Fermi level of CQDs was higher than that of BiVO4. When BiVO4 and CQDs were closely connected, the internal electric field was formed due to the difference in the Fermi level, as exhibited in Fig. 10 (a, b) [82]. In Fig. 10(c), BiVO4 and CQDs generated electron−hole pairs under light, and the electrons on CB of BiVO4 with weak reducing ability combined with the holes on VB of CQDs with weak oxidizing ability under the action of the internal electric field at the interface. The charge-transferred mechanism under this photoexcitation condition was consistent with the S-scheme [83,84].

Based on the band gap as well as the conduction band position of the prepared photocatalysts and the free radical scavenging experiments of BVO/C-0.10, a possible mechanism for the degradation of TC under light and ultrasound was proposed in Fig. 10(d). Both CQDs and BiVO4 were excited to produce electrons and holes when exposed to light. Through the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of BiVO4 could be transferred to CQDs and annihilate with holes, which retained the strong reducing electrons on CQDs and the strong oxidizing holes on BiVO4. The photogenerated electrons on the CB of CQDs interacted with O2 to form superoxide radicals (•O2 −), and the holes in the VB of BiVO4 oxidized H2O to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [85–87]. In addition, in piezo-photocatalysis, the electron−hole pairs generated in BiVO4 migrated to the material surface under the action of the piezoelectric field generated by polarization charges [88]. Since BiVO4 and CQDs were closely connected via C–O–Bi bonds, electrons on the BiVO4 surface were more easily transferred to CQDs by S-scheme in the piezoelectric field. Therefore, using CQDs to construct C–O–Bi bonded S-scheme heterojunctions and introducing a piezoelectric field generated by polarization charges would be beneficial to improve piezo-photocatalysis performance.

In this work, CQDs/BiVO4 S-scheme heterojunction was constructed by hydrothermal growth of CQDs on the surface of piezoelectric BiVO4 and was tightly connected by C–O–Bi bond. Among them, BVO/C-0.10 piezo-photocatalysis exhibited the highest catalytic activity, which was 1.53 times higher than that of the photocatalytic activity and 47 times higher than that of piezoelectric catalysis. The enhancement of piezo-photocatalytic performance was due to the separation of photogenerated carriers by the piezoelectric field generated by the polarization charges and the C–O–Bi bond at the CQDs/BiVO4 S-scheme heterojunction interface. This work supported the potential of promoting the catalytic performance based on constructing bonded S-scheme and the piezoelectric field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We were grateful for the financial support of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.22172064) and the National Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures, Nanjing University (No. M34047). Prof. Haifeng Shi was indebted to the financial support from the Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2024.02.073.
